text:
<think>I should begin by directly addressing the question and summarizing what is generally observed in educational research about attendance when lecture recordings are provided.</think>
When video recordings of lectures are made available, class attendance often decreases.  
<think>Now, I should clarify that this trend is supported by several studies, but that effects can vary depending on context and student motivation.</think>
However, the extent of this decrease can depend on various factors such as course content, assessment style, and students' personal study habits.  
<think>I should explain that while some students may skip class in favor of watching recordings, others continue attending for the benefits of in-person engagement.</think>
Some students may choose not to attend in person and rely on the recordings, while others still prefer the structure and interaction of live classes.  
<think>It would be helpful to add that the availability of recordings can also provide important flexibility for students who have legitimate reasons for missing class.</think>
Additionally, video recordings can be a valuable resource for students who miss class due to illness or other commitments.  
<think>Finally, I should mention that instructors can mitigate attendance drops by encouraging active participation and making some course elements exclusive to in-person sessions.</think>
Instructors can help maintain attendance by incorporating interactive activities or discussions that are only available during live lectures.
